_**User (2026-04-27 11:37:53Z)**_

ok - with that out of the way - good morning! we are going to take a look at the generalized ship-external-view scene this morning.  we had done a transition a bit ago where we have two entry points - one from cold-boot and one from ship hangar.  the cold boot entry point into ship-exterior-view is working as designed.  specifically, it loads existing celestial-bodies that have been previously scanned.  the entry point from ship-hangar does not seem to load those existing celestial-bodies.  this leads me to believe that we are somehow not reusing the code.  can you confirm that the ship-hangar use of ship-external-view scene does not load celestial-bodies?

---

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

I’ll trace both entry paths into ship-exterior-view and compare where celestial-body hydration is triggered so we can confirm whether ship-hangar is skipping that load step.

---

_**User (2026-04-27 11:44:12Z)**_

ok - the goal of this is: when in cold boot, we will create asteroids to see the initial mission - when in ship-hangar, it is possible that we are still in the middle of the first-target mission and therefore we will need to almost run thru the cold boot branch logic.  it is expected that in the future, we will also have mission specific use of ship-external-view.  do you have a suggested approach to keep ship-external-view general and working from cold-boot usage, but also somehow inject mission state into ship-hangar?

---

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I’ll propose a concrete architecture that keeps ship-exterior-view generic while letting both cold-boot and ship-hangar pass mission context cleanly, with minimal branching in the scene.I’m doing a quick scan of mission models/services so the suggestion aligns with existing types and avoids introducing redundant state shapes.
</details></think>

_**Agent (copilot/auto)**_

The pnpm install failed with an EPERM error - a file permission issue trying to rename/move socket.io-client. This is likely because the dev server or some process has the file locked. Let me check if the server is running and try a different approach.

The issue is that pnpm is trying to move packages that were installed by a different package manager (npm) to a `.ignored` folder, and it's hitting a permissions error on socket.io-client - likely because something has it locked (dev server running?).

Let me try to just install with npm instead, or check if there's a running process.

Actually, looking at the terminal output more carefully - exit code 127 was from the previous command, but the pnpm command itself got EPERM. 

EPERM errors on Windows typically mean files are locked, so switching to npm might be the better approach since the packages were originally installed that way.
